title: Amalia Vradi
text: I’m a Data Scientist who likes to work with data and Machine Learning. I am part of the innovation team at Sixense Satellite, a company based in Barcelona that uses remote sensing data for monitoring motion and stability.

title: How a neural network works
text:
Deep learning is a subset of machine learning that uses neural networks to model and solve complex problems. In this post, we will take a look at how a neural network works and how it can be used to solve real-world problems using PyTorch.
A neural network is a collection of artificial neurons that are connected together to form a network. Each neuron takes in a set of inputs, performs a series of mathematical operations on them, and then generates an output. The output of one neuron is then passed as input to the next neuron in the network.
The input to a neural network is typically a set of features that describe a problem or task. For example, in an image classification task, the input features might be the pixel values of an image. In a natural language processing task, the input features might be the words in a sentence.
Here is an example of how to define a simple neural network with one hidden layer in PyTorch:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class Net(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super(Net, self).__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(100, 64)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(64, 10)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = torch.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = self.fc2(x)
        return x

net = Net()
In this example, we first import the necessary modules from PyTorch. Then we create a custom class that inherits from the nn.Module class. Inside the class, we define the layers of our network as instances of the nn.Linear class and specify the number of neurons in the layer and the size of the input for each layer. We also define a forward() method that specifies how the input is passed through the network and processed by each layer.
Once the input features have been passed through the network, they are processed by the neurons in each layer. The first layer of neurons, called the input layer, takes in the raw input features and passes them on to the next layer. The next layer, called the hidden layer, performs a series of mathematical operations on the input and passes the results on to the next layer. This process continues through one or more hidden layers until the final layer, called the output layer, generates the final prediction or output of the network.
The mathematical operations that are performed by the neurons in each layer include a dot product of the input with a set of weights and an activation function. The dot product is used to calculate the weighted sum of the inputs, while the activation function is used to introduce non-linearity into the network. This non-linearity allows the network to model complex relationships between the input features and the output. In this example, we use the torch.relu() function as the activation function.
Once the output is generated, it is compared to the true output or label and the error is calculated using a loss function. This error is then used to update the weights of the network through a process called backpropagation. This process is repeated multiple times with different sets of input data until the network reaches a sufficient level of accuracy.
Here is an example of how to compile the model and train it using a sample dataset in PyTorch:
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(net.parameters(), lr=0.01)

for epoch in range(5):
# Forward pass
output = net(x_train)
loss = criterion(output, y_train)
In this example, we use the nn.CrossEntropyLoss() as the loss function. We also use the torch.optim.SGD optimizer and specify the network’s parameters and the learning rate. In the training loop, we pass the input data through the network to get the output, then calculate the loss using the output and the true labels. We then use the optimizer to perform backpropagation and update the weights of the network.
After training, the neural network can be used to make predictions on new, unseen data. This can be done by passing the new data through the network and generating an output. Here is an example of how to use the trained model to make predictions on new data in PyTorch:
y_pred = net(x_test)
In this example, we pass the x_test data through the trained network to generate predictions, the resulting y_pred variable contains the predicted output for each input in the test set.
The trained network can be used to solve a wide range of problems, including image classification, natural language processing, and even game playing. PyTorch provides a flexible and powerful framework for implementing and experimenting with neural networks.
In conclusion, a neural network is a collection of artificial neurons that are connected together to form a network. Input is passed through the network, processed by the neurons in each layer, and output is generated. By adjusting the weights, the network learns to make accurate predictions on new data. Neural networks have been proven to be powerful tools for solving complex problems in a wide range of fields, and PyTorch provides a great ecosystem for implementing and experimenting with neural networks.
